title: Polymer clay leaf pendant
subtitle: A textured leaf pendant shaped from a real leaf and baked hard
excerpt: A polymer clay leaf pendant is pressed over a real leaf to transfer the vein pattern, cut to shape, pierced for a jump ring, and baked at 130°C for 30 minutes. The leaf is peeled away after pressing and the clay bakes to a hard, durable finish. No kiln is needed. The pendant can be left in natural clay colours or painted with acrylic before sealing.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay leaf texture technique. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: picks up vein impressions from real leaves more accurately than air-dry clay because it does not shrink during
. The vein is pressed in, the leaf is peeled away, and the clay is cut to the leaf outline before baking. The result is a pendant that shows the exact venation of the leaf it was pressed from.

attrs.body: Choose leaves with raised veins on the underside: oak, maple, ivy, sage, and rose all work well. Large flat leaves with prominent midribs are the easiest. Very smooth leaves like laurel give little texture.
attrs.title: Which leaves work

items.name: Pottery rolling pin (or small glass jar)

content.text: Step 1: Condition and roll a thin slab

Condition the clay until soft. Roll to 3 mm thick on a smooth surface using the rolling pin or a small glass jar. A pendant this thin shows the vein texture well and is the right weight for jewellery.
Step 2: Press the leaf onto the clay

Place the leaf underside-down onto the clay slab. Roll firmly over it with the rolling pin, applying even pressure across the whole leaf. Peel the leaf back slowly from one tip: the vein impression should be clearly visible in the clay.
Step 3: Cut to shape and pierce

Using the craft knife, cut around the leaf outline or cut a simplified leaf shape that captures the main silhouette. Cut cleanly by pressing straight down rather than dragging. Pierce a hole at the top of the leaf stem, 3 mm from the edge, using the needle tool. The hole should be large enough for the jump ring.

Place the leaf on baking paper on a dedicated tray. Preheat the oven to 130°C, confirmed with the thermometer. Bake for 20 minutes (thinner pieces cure faster than beads). Cool completely before handling. Attach a jump ring through the pierced hole and thread onto a chain. Seal with a brush-on polymer clay varnish if a glossy finish is wanted, or leave matte.
Mix two clay colours together incompletely before rolling for a two-tone marbled pendant.
Paint the cured pendant with metallic acrylic, gold on a dark clay base gives an antique effect.

attrs.intro: Problems when making the leaf pendant.

items.fix: Turn the leaf over: veins are on the underside. Roll firmly with the rolling pin, you need more pressure than feels natural.
items.cause: The leaf was pressed smooth-side-down, or pressure was too light.
items.symptom: Leaf impression barely visible after pressing

items.fix: Condition the clay until it bends without cracking before rolling. If a crack forms during baking, fill it with a small amount of fresh clay and re-bake for 15 minutes.
items.cause: Clay not conditioned thoroughly, leaving air pockets.

attrs.heading: Common problems
